coredata.dc:description: Silicon, which is gaining prominence as a potential anode material, exhibits long-term cycle stability issues owing to volume changes during cycling. This study presents a meticulously designed Si anode that employs intense pulsed light (IPL) technology to simultaneously reduce carbon additives and construct an effective carbon quantum dot (CQD)-derived binder system. IPL, as a light-material interaction technique, offers a novel approach for selective heat treatment with remarkably short processing durations on the order of milliseconds. By comprehensively considering the geometric structure, optical characteristics, and thermal properties of each electrode component, heat transfer bridge (HTB) materials are introduced to ensure a homogeneous depth-directional heat treatment and to minimize binder decomposition in the IPL process. By employing HTB materials, the binder can reach a controlled target temperature for the condensation reaction between poly (acrylic acid) and the CQDs. Simultaneously, higher heat generation in the conductive carbon than in the binder leads to selective additional carbon reduction. This unique approach provides selective heat treatment, which cannot be achieved using conventional methods, resulting in a Si anode with excellent cyclic stability and rate capability. The versatility of the IPL technology is further demonstrated by applying it to relatively inexpensive Si microparticles.

(a) UV–vis spectroscopy of the electrode components. (b) Typical thermal conductivity of bulk Si [64], the individual in-plane thermal conductivity of graphene [59,60], the individual thermal conductivity of CNTs [61,62], and measured thermal conductivity of the PAA and the PAA-CQD films (10 wt% CQDs) at room temperature. (c) Heat transfer mechanisms during IPL heat treatment and conventional slow heat treatment.

(c) Cross-sectional Raman mapping analysis of the SN-HTB electrodes before and after the IPL treatment (2, 4, and 6 J cm−2).

(a) FT-IR spectra of the SN-HTB electrodes before and after IPL irradiation. (b) TGA curves for the SN-HTB electrodes prepared using various IPL irradiation intensities and SN-HTB electrodes annealed in a tube furnace at different temperatures. (c) Schematic illustration of the PAA-CQD-crosslinked network binder. (d) PAA amounts in the SN-HTB electrodes prepared using various IPL irradiation intensities and annealing temperatures. The inset schematic image illustrates the measurement of electrical conductivity for the as-prepared electrode using the four-point probe method. (b) Electrical conductivity of the AHNS-rGO film prepared using various IPL irradiation intensities. The inset image shows the as-prepared AHNS-rGO film fabricated using vacuum filtration. (c) FE-SEM images of the SN-HTB electrodes before and after IPL irradiation. (d) Schematic illustrating the changes in the electrode binder and conductive carbon agents according to the IPL irradiation and annealing conditions.

The inset image shows the average CEs of the SN, SN-HTB Bare, SN-HTB 4 J cm−2, and SN-HTB 6 J cm−2 electrodes. (c) SEM images of the SN-HTB Bare electrode and (d) SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode after 50 cycles. (e) Discharge capacity retention and (f) ICE of SM-HTB electrodes with various IPL irradiation intensities at a specific current of 0.5 A/g.

head.ce:title: Intense pulsed light-induced millisecond selective heat treatment for high-performance silicon anodes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study proposes a novel method that uses intense pulsed light (IPL) technology for the simultaneous carbon reduction and binder modification of silicon anodes. By providing a rational design based on material properties and battery engineering, and employing an IPL, this approach offers new insights into selective heat treatment, paving the way for innovative advancements in battery electrode engineering.

Silicon, which is gaining prominence as a potential anode material, exhibits long-term cycle stability issues owing to volume changes during cycling. This study presents a meticulously designed Si anode that employs intense pulsed light (IPL) technology to simultaneously reduce carbon additives and construct an effective carbon quantum dot (CQD)-derived binder system. IPL, as a light-material interaction technique, offers a novel approach for selective heat treatment with remarkably short processing durations on the order of milliseconds. By comprehensively considering the geometric structure, optical characteristics, and thermal properties of each electrode component, heat transfer bridge (HTB) materials are introduced to ensure a homogeneous depth-directional heat treatment and to minimize binder decomposition in the IPL process. By employing HTB materials, the binder can reach a controlled target temperature for the condensation reaction between poly (acrylic acid) and the CQDs. Simultaneously, higher heat generation in the conductive carbon than in the binder leads to selective additional carbon reduction. This unique approach provides selective heat treatment, which cannot be achieved using conventional methods, resulting in a Si anode with excellent cyclic stability and rate capability. The versatility of the IPL technology is further demonstrated by applying it to relatively inexpensive Si microparticles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the world moves towards zero emissions, the demand for and significance of Li-ion batteries (LIBs) are rapidly increasing, especially in the electric vehicle (EV) market [1–4]. Maximizing the energy density of LIBs within the limited space and weight of EVs is a substantial challenge for achieving the driving range goal of EVs [5]. Despite the material- and electrode-level innovations over the past decades, achieving a higher energy density remains challenging. Silicon has received considerable attention as an alternative to graphite, which has a limited specific capacity (∼372 mAh g−1), for use as an anode in LIBs [6,7]. Silicon has been evaluated as an outstanding active material candidate for next-generation anodes with excellent price competitiveness because of its terrestrial abundance, high theoretical specific capacity (>3500 mAh g−1) [8,9], and relatively low working potential (<0.5 V vs. Li/Li+) [10]. However, the high-capacity of Si electrodes poses challenges in maintaining long-term cycle stability. The Si storage mechanism based on alloying chemistry ensures a high specific capacity with a high Li atom-to-host atom ratio (∼4:1) [11]. However, the reversible store/release capability of Si for Li at the atomic scale inevitably causes volume changes in Si during electrochemical cycling, leading to structural degeneration at the electrode level. Undesirable structural changes, such as mechanical cracking, unstable and thick solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation, and electrical contact loss owing to particle isolation, mainly degrade Si electrodes, limiting their widespread adoption as major anode active materials [12–14].

Numerous strategies have been proposed to address the challenges arising from the intrinsic volume changes of Si during (de)lithiation. These efforts have mainly focused on the nanostructuring of Si and the development of polymeric binder systems [15–22]. For nanostructuring, 0-dimensional (0-D), 1-dimensional (1-D), and 2-dimensional (2-D) morphology-nanostructured Si have been successfully fabricated using various techniques, such as chemical vapor deposition [17,18,23], physical vapor deposition [24,25], and magnesiothermic reduction [19,26–28]. Liu et al. [29] investigated the critical diameter for the fracture behavior of Si and found no mechanical instability in Si particles with diameters < 150 nm. Introducing nanosized Si effectively prevented pulverization, resulting in a significantly reduced long-term capacity fading, as compared with microsized Si. However, the inherently high specific surface area of nanosized Si leads to irreversible reactions, such as SEI formation, resulting in a low initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) [30,31]. Additionally, a relatively large amount of conductive agent is required to achieve interparticle junctions owing to its high particle boundaries [32]. Alternatively, 1-D and 2-D carbon additives, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), can provide effective conductive networks for Si interparticles [32–34]. From the perspective of cost-effectiveness and mass production, most commercialized electrode fabrication processes involve the preparation of a slurry by mixing the electrode materials (active material and conductive agent) with a binder. The slurry is then cast onto a current collector using a roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing system. Notably, aqueous binders capable of creating strong covalent or hydrogen bonds with Si particles have been widely employed to improve the structural endurance of Si anodes during cycling [35–38]. However, the 1-D spatial domain of linear polymers may not be sufficient to withstand the substantial stresses induced by the volumetric variations of Si [39]. The ability of Si to accommodate volumetric expansion can be enhanced using molecular engineering approaches, thereby establishing a more resilient three-dimensional network structure with a crosslinking system [39–44]. However, when these water-soluble binder systems and Si nanoparticles (SiNPs) are considered during processing, a quasi-trade-off relationship arises between the dispersibility and conductivity of 1-D and 2-D conductive carbon. This presents a challenge for reducing the electrode resistance [45]. That is, the use of highly conductive carbon before slurry production causes aggregation because of its poor dispersibility, making it difficult to cast the slurry uniformly. Additionally, it is almost impossible to improve the electrical conductivity of the electrode using typical thermal annealing methods after slurry casting because the temperature required for carbon reduction (>300 °C) to achieve a high conductivity is too high for the binder. Typically, polymer binders decompose at 300 °C.

To address these challenges, Si anodes that enable the reduction of carbon materials and simultaneously introduce the construction of an effective carbon quantum dot (CQD)-derived crosslinked binder system with a processing time of a few milliseconds were meticulously designed based on the understanding of the material properties and intense pulsed light (IPL) technology. IPL is a novel technology that converts light energy into thermal energy by irradiating a sample with visible light in the range of 380–950 nm under atmospheric conditions [46–49]. This technology is suitable for commercialization because of its robust compatibility with the R2R process [50,51]. Unlike traditional slow thermal annealing, the short heat-treatment time (∼milliseconds) of IPL can induce a local temperature gradient, allowing for selective heat treatment [52]. This selective heat treatment can minimize the thermal damage to the polymeric binder and achieve the target temperature for forming a crosslinking network binder. Simultaneously, higher heat energy can be applied to the conductive carbon additive, which has excellent heat absorptivity and conductivity, as compared to the polymeric binder, resulting in additional carbon reduction. However, a comprehensive understanding of the geometric structure and the optical and thermal properties of each component is essential to successfully employ IPL for heterogeneous electrodes composed of various particles and a polymeric binder. Additionally, the directional nature of IPL can result in nonuniform heat treatment in the depth direction, forming a thermally conductive network crucial for the uniform heat treatment of the entire electrode. Considering the important properties mentioned above, a unique CQD-derived binder system and additional reduction of conductive carbon additives were concurrently achieved across the electrode using IPL-induced selective heat treatment, which could not be achieved using conventional methods, resulting in excellent cycle stability and rate capability of the electrode. Furthermore, the versatility of IPL technology was confirmed by its application to electrodes composed of relatively inexpensive Si microparticles (SiMPs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped CQDs via hydrothermal reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped CQDs were synthesized using a bottom-up method. Briefly, citric acid (Sigma Aldrich, 1.05 g) and urea (Sigma Aldrich, 0.9 g) were dissolved in 100 ml DI water. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the prepared solution was purified. Unreacted residues and very small particles were removed using dialysis, and large particles were removed using a syringe filter. The obtained solution was powdered using freeze-drying.

The synthesis method employed in this study is described in our previous study [53]. In detail, a diluted graphene oxide (GO) dispersion (2 mg ml−1) was prepared by adding DI water to commercially acquired GO (N002-PS, Angstrong Materials). Subsequently, ammonia (6 mg) was slowly added to an AHNS solution (5 mL; 20 wt%), and the solution was dispersed using a bath sonicator. The as-prepared AHNS solution was then slowly added to the GO solution using a glass pipette to prepare a mixed solution. This mixed solution was heated at 70 °C for 24 h to allow the AHNS to react with the epoxy group of GO and functionalize it. Subsequently, the AHNS-functionalized GO was reduced by heating at 100 °C for 20 h using hydrazine monohydrate. The prepared mixed solution was washed several times with DI water to remove the unreacted materials. The washed AHNS-rGO was redispersed in ethanol and used in subsequent processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the SiNP/micro heat transfer bridge (HTB) material composite
ce:section.ce:para: SiNPs (CN vision, particle size 100–200 nm; 1.5 g), CNTs (US Research Nanomaterials; 0.1 g) dispersible in ethanol, and an AHNS-rGO solution (100 ml in ethanol) were mixed and then ultrasonicated to achieve homogeneous dispersion. This homogeneous solution was heated at 80 °C while stirring to remove the ethanol. The mixture was then placed in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 4 h for thorough drying. CNTs and AHNS-rGO were used as micro HTB materials. The lengths of the CNTs are in the range of 300 nm to 5 µm, with outside diameters ranging from 20 to 50 nm (Fig. S1a). The lateral dimensions of the AHNS-rGO flakes are in the range of 1–2 µm (Fig. S1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of SiNP and SiMP electrodes with HTB materials
ce:section.ce:para: Before fabricating the electrodes, PAA (Sigma Aldrich, M.W. 450000) and CQDs were prepared at a mass ratio of 90:10 by mixing them in DI water under simple stirring. To fabricate the slurry for the SiNP electrode with HTB materials, the SiNP/micro HTB composite was mixed with Super P (TIMCAL Graphite & Carbon) and the as-prepared PAA-CQD binder solution at a mass ratio of 85:5:10 (excluding the solvent weight of the solution). The slurry was then cast on Cu foil, and the slurry-coated electrodes were dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 6 h. The mass level of the active materials (SiNPs) was approximately 1 mg cm−2. For the fabrication of the slurry for the SiMP electrode with HTB materials, SiMPs (Alfa Aesar, particle size 1–5 µm) were mixed with Super P, bulk conductive graphite (provided by Hyundai Motor), CNTs, and the as-prepared PAA-CQD binder solution at a mass ratio of 85:2.5:2.5:2.5:7.5 (excluding the solvent weight of the solution). Subsequently, the slurry was cast on Cu foil, and the resulting slurry-coated SiMP electrodes with HTB materials were dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 6 h. The mass of the SiMPs used in this fabrication was approximately 0.9 mg cm−2.

IPL irradiation was performed using a system that included a xenon lamp, lamp head, power supply, and pulse generator (myPET-LM200; MetaSOL, Korea). A reflector was built into the lamp head to focus the flashed white light during the arc discharge of the xenon lamp. Additionally, a chilling system was included to prevent damage to the lamp head owing to the high temperatures. The pulse control of the IPL was investigated using a power supply and pulse generator, and the energy density of the pulse was adjusted by modifying the charge voltage, pulse irradiation time, and distance between the sample and lamp head. The details of the IPL system and pulse parameters are shown in Fig. S11a, b. Before the IPL irradiation, the energy densities were measured using an energy meter (Nova II, NovaCentrix). The as-prepared SiNP and SiMP electrodes with HTB materials were then irradiated using IPL at various energy densities with one pulse (10 ms irradiation time) at room temperature under ambient conditions. The distance between the electrodes and the lamp head was 40 mm. The SiNP electrodes integrated with microscale HTB materials (CNTs and AHNS-rGO) and a PAA-CQD binder are designated as SN-HTB X J cm−2 (where X =2, 3, 4, 5, and 6) based on the applied IPL energy density. The SiMP electrode integrated with microscale HTB materials (CNTs and bulk graphite) and a PAA-CQD binder is denoted as SM-HTB.

The SN-HTB electrodes were heated in a tube furnace to post-treat the electrodes using the conventional slow annealing method. The heating process was conducted at 200, 400, and 600 °C for 1 h under an Ar flow rate of 500 mL min−1 and a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. Subsequently, the furnace was allowed to cool to room temperature and the annealed electrodes were used for subsequent analysis.

2032-type coin cells were assembled in an Ar-filled glove box to investigate the electrochemical properties. A monolayer polypropylene membrane (Celgard 2400) was used as the separator. Lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6, PANAX ETEC; 1 M) in ethylene carbonate:diethylene carbonate:vinylene carbonate:fluoroethylene carbonate in a volume ratio of 44.5:44.5:10:1 was used as the electrolyte for the reference and counter electrodes. Galvanostatic charge–discharge tests were performed using an electrochemical workstation (WBCS3000S, WonATech). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of half-cells was conducted in the frequency range from 1 MHz to 10 mHz.

The UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra of the samples were measured at 25 °C using a spectrometer (Lambda 650S, Perkin Elmer) in the scanning range of 300–850 nm. The thermal conductivity was measured at 25 °C using a light flash apparatus (LFA 467, Netzsch). Cross-sectional polishing (IB-19510CP, JEOL, 270 Tokyo, Japan) and Raman spectroscopy (DXR3xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific) were conducted to investigate the degree of reduction of the conductive carbon agent across the electrode cross-section. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, SDT Q600, TA Instruments) was performed to estimate the amount of binder in the electrodes. The surface areas of the SiNPs and SiMPs were estimated using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller method (Autosorb IQ, Quantuachrome). The morphologies of the samples were observed using Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Verios G4 UC, FEI). The electrical conductivities of the samples were evaluated using a single-configuration four-point probe method with a resistivity measurement system (Keithley, Tektronix). For post-cycling characterization, the coin cells were disassembled and the anodes were removed, washed three times with dimethyl carbonate, and dried at 50 °C under vacuum for 6 h. The dried anodes were further characterized using FE-SEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher Scientific Messtechnik) was performed to understand the chemical bonding environment of carbon. Field emission electron probe microanalysis (FE-EPMA, JXA-8500F, JEOL) was conducted to explore the elemental distribution in the electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrafast selective heating process using IPL technology and a rational electrode design
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the electrode can be photothermally heated using visible light emitted from a xenon lamp in the IPL system. The pulse duration, representing the on-time of the xenon lamp light source, can be adjusted from a few milliseconds to hundreds of milliseconds, depending on the purpose. Table S1 shows the comparison of conventional heat treatment and IPL technology. Owing to this short IPL irradiation time, the electrode experiences instantaneous superheating with a high annealing rate of 104–107 K s−1, and then rapidly cools to room temperature [52,54,55]. Consequently, IPL, unlike conventional slow heat treatment methods, is highly compatible with the commercial battery R2R manufacturing system, which operates at a rolling speed of 60 m min−1 or higher because of its fast processing speed and substrate-by-substrate processing under atmospheric conditions. Interestingly, the heating mechanism of the electrode using IPL is based on photothermal annealing. Because it involves an anisotropic process with the directionality of light irradiation, the temperature change of the materials in the electrode is significantly influenced by their optical and thermal properties. The combination of “directionality” and “extremely short processing time” in IPL enables deviation in the degree of heat treatment along the depth axis of the electrode film, allowing for selective thermal processing.

HTB materials were introduced into the electrode to achieve homogeneous depth-directional heat treatment of the electrode and to minimize binder decomposition during the IPL process. Effective HTB materials for Si electrodes have several requirements, namely (i) electrochemically beneficial or stable, (ii) high thermal and electrical conductivity, (iii) high visible light absorptivity, (iv) high dispersibility in the slurry for homogeneous distribution, and (v) structural properties that enhance interparticle contacts. Therefore, CNTs, rGO, and CQDs were selected as HTB materials. In particular, the introduction of 1-D and 2-D conductive carbon (CNTs and rGO) facilitates electrical and thermal percolation more effectively than 0-D particles, such as Super P conductive carbon (Fig. 1b). These CNTs and rGOs create numerous junctions between the electrode particles, thereby establishing microscale thermal conduction pathways and alleviating undesirable thermally discrete phenomena in the depth direction of the electrode. Notably, the formation of rGO aggregates is common during the slurry manufacturing process using water as the solvent.

Since the dispersibility of the HTB material significantly affects the heat transfer mechanism during the IPL treatment, AHNS-rGO, introduced in our previous study, was employed as the HTB material [53]. AHNS-rGO exhibits excellent dispersibility in aqueous media and a remarkable electrical conductivity of 71.6 S m−1, as ascertained using the four-point probe technique. Notably, 0-D CQDs do not provide microscale thermal conduction pathways but exist in a state coated with a binder on the surface of the individual particles. They act as nanoscale pathways for heat transfer between particles, thereby suppressing damage to the binder. The CQDs serve as crosslinkers, forming a network binder via a condensation reaction with poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) during the IPL process, which can accommodate the volume expansion/contraction of Si during cycling. The CQDs were synthesized using a bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis. Fig. S2a shows digital images of the CQD powder obtained after freeze-drying. The synthesized CQDs exhibited a bright blue color, which was attributed to the π- π* transition of the conjugated C=C band of the CQDs under ultraviolet (UV) light (Fig. S2b) [56]. The TEM image in Fig. S2c shows that the CQDs had a spherical morphology with diameters of 4–6 nm. Fig. S2d shows the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the CQDs and PAA. The absorption bands of the CQDs at 3000–3500 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H, and the peaks related to the N-H in-plane bending vibration and C-N stretching vibration at 1579 and 1402 cm−1 (1254 cm−1) indicated the presence of multiple hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the CQDs. Moreover, PAA exhibited broad absorption bands at 3140 and 2955 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the O-H and C-H bonds, respectively. Distinctive peaks were observed at 1711, 1455, and 1252 cm−1, corresponding to the C=O, –CH2 plane, and C-OH stretching modes, respectively. These FT-IR results imply that a condensation reaction can occur via the surface functional groups (–OH and –NH2) present on the CQDs and the –COOH group of PAA, forming a covalently crosslinked network structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and thermal properties of the electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the heat treatment mechanism underlying the IPL process, it is imperative to understand the light-absorption process on the electrode surface and heat transfer. Since the electrode is composed of heterogeneous materials, exploring their properties, particularly those of the individual components, is indispensable. Considering that the dominant wavelength range of light emitted by a xenon lamp during the IPL process is 380–900 nm (Fig. S4), UV–vis spectroscopic analyses were conducted within the wavelength range of 300–850 nm to investigate the absorption properties of each material comprising the electrode (Fig. 2 a). Diluted solutions (0.001 wt%) in deionized (DI) water were employed for these tests. The 2-D nature of the AHNS-rGO material has a high surface area, which culminated in a heightened absorption intensity over the entire wavelength spectrum. CNTs and SiNPs exhibited lower absorption levels within the 300–850 nm wavelength range than AHNS-rGO. Conversely, owing to its transparent and colorless nature, PAA exhibited no absorption in any spectral range. The nonabsorbing property of PAA underscores its inability to be directly photothermally heated during the IPL process.

The distinct absorption characteristics of each electrode material for light from the xenon lamp cause the generation of different local thermal energies. To gain a comprehensive understanding of the detailed heat generation and conduction in these electrodes under IPL irradiation, it is necessary to understand their basic principles. When irradiated with light corresponding to the absorption wavelength of a material, photons with energies higher than the optical bandgap are absorbed, leading to the photoexcitation of electrons [57]. This energy from the excited electrons is transferred to the lattice of the material through electron–phonon coupling, ultimately producing thermal energy. Heat conduction in solid materials occurs via two factors, namely, acoustic phonons and electrons. The thermal conductivity of a solid material can be calculated using Equation (1) [58]:
(1)
K =
K p
+
K e
where Kp and Ke are the phonon and electron contributions, respectively. Heat transfer by phonons is dominant in carbon materials, such as graphene, CNT, and diamonds. In particular, graphene (>2500 W mK−1) [59,60] and CNTs (>2000 W mK−1) [61,62] have very high thermal conductivities owing to their heat transfer ability through the sp2 lattice. In contrast, most polymers have extremely low thermal conductivities (0.1–0.5 W mK−1) due to their intrinsic amorphous structures [63]. Therefore, in our designed electrode, the primary pathway for heat transfer from the irradiated surface to the Cu current collector at the electrode base within time-constrained milliseconds was facilitated using CNTs and AHNS-rGO. To understand the thermal properties of the binder in the proposed electrode system, the thermal conductivities of PAA and PAA-CQD films were measured at 25 °C. As shown in Fig. 2b, the thermal conductivities of the PAA and PAA-CQD films were 0.67 and 1.18 W mK−1, respectively. The approximately two-fold increase in the thermal conductivity of the PAA-CQD films, as compared with that of the PAA film, can be attributed to the CQDs acting as a filler, forming a pathway for heat transfer within the PAA polymer matrix. However, because the weight percentage of CQDs in the PAA-CQD composite film was 10 %, the PAA-CQD film still exhibited a significantly lower thermal conductivity than the other carbon materials. In a film composed solely of PAA and CQDs, the heat-transfer efficacy may be constrained by the discrete distribution of the CQDs. However, within the electrode, the CQDs existed on the particle surface of the electrode materials in tandem with PAA, thereby effectively functioning as nanoscale HTB-interlinking particles (Fig. 2c). From a comprehensive perspective, the predominant heat transfer occurs effectively through the CNTs or AHNS-rGO, which possess larger-dimensional characteristics. Additionally, localized heat transfer can occur among the nanoscale CQDs. The heat treatment process in this study was conducted using IPL for an on-time duration of 10 ms, which is significantly shorter than conventional slow heat treatment methods, which usually take several hours to attain thermal equilibrium. Owing to the extremely short duration of the heat treatment process, thermal diffusion to PAA, which has low thermal conductivity, was effectively minimized. This mitigates thermal damage to the polymeric binder while enabling the selective and efficient heat treatment of the electrode particles, such as conductive carbon. The thermal, optical, and functional characteristics of each electrode composition are summarized in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Realization of consistent thermal processing throughout the depth dimension
ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring uniform heat treatment throughout the electrode is a critical consideration in the context of IPL technology. Nonuniform heat treatment in the depth direction can arise from the inherent directional processing characteristics of the light-based method, which requires a consistent heat distribution throughout the lowermost electrode. Therefore, Raman spectroscopy was performed to investigate the uniform heating capability of the designed electrodes using HTB materials in the IPL process. The Ig/Id ratio in Raman spectroscopy indicates the crystallinity attributed to the sp2 bonds of the carbon material, which reflects the degree of reduction in the carbon material [65]. Even when IPL irradiation is performed under atmospheric conditions, conductive carbon can be further reduced. The instantaneous creation of a reducing atmosphere during IPL irradiation can be attributed to several factors. First, rapid IPL treatment limits O2 supply owing to insufficient atmospheric convection. Second, photochemical or thermal reactions during the IPL treatment promptly generate gases, such as H2O or CO2, on the surface of the electrode, establishing a local high-pressure zone that restricts external atmospheric contact. As a result, a reducing atmosphere forms, facilitating carbon reduction despite the ambient processing conditions. Consequently, monitoring the degree of reduction of conductive carbon in the depth direction serves as a reliable indicator for evaluating the uniformity of the heat treatment throughout the depth profile during IPL processing. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the Ig/Id values of the conductive carbon materials used in this study, including Super P, CNTs, and AHNS-rGO, were approximately 0.9. Additionally, cross-sectional polishing Raman mapping analyses were conducted to evaluate the degree of carbon reduction throughout the depth of the electrode (Fig. 3c). Given the significant influence of the electrode density on the heat transfer process via IPL, IPL irradiation was performed after the calendaring process to mitigate any effects and variables arising from the electrode density. After the calendaring process, the density of the SN-HTB Bare electrodes was approximately 0.75 g cc−1. Fig. 3c shows that the IPL irradiation intensity of 2 J cm−2 for the SN-HTB 2 J cm−2 electrode was insufficient to induce further reduction of the conductive carbon. This led to an Ig/Id value comparable to that of the untreated electrode, signifying minimal alteration of the carbon structure in the electrode following IPL treatment. In contrast, as the IPL irradiation intensity increased from 4 to 6 J cm−2, significant enhancements in the Ig/Id values were observed. This enhancement suggests that the sp2 lattice of carbon was augmented through additional conductive carbon reduction facilitated by the IPL-induced thermal treatment. Moreover, the absence of significant variations in the depth direction suggests that heat treatment using the IPL irradiation method is feasible for the HTB-integrated SN-HTB electrode with a thickness of approximately 13 µm.

FT-IR measurements were conducted to investigate the chemical changes in the PAA-CQD binder system according to the IPL irradiation strength (Fig. 4 a). The FT-IR spectrum of the SN-HTB electrode before IPL irradiation revealed a C=O stretching band of the carboxylic acid group in PAA at a wavenumber of 1711 cm−1. Upon IPL irradiation at 3 and 4 J cm−2, the C=O stretching band shifted to 1716 cm−1. This shift was attributed to thermal energy transfer through the CQDs, leading to condensation reactions between the hydroxyl groups of the CQDs and the carboxylic acid groups of the neighboring PAA, forming ester groups. This condensation reaction leads to the formation of covalent bonds between the CQDs and PAA, ultimately creating a chemically crosslinked network. The condensation reaction between hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups typically occurs under heating at 150–180 °C in inert environments [66–69]. It is worth noting that the IPL technique facilitates these condensation reactions without requiring specific atmospheric conditions. Remarkably, the C=O stretching band was no longer observed at IPL irradiation intensities exceeding 5 J cm−2. During the IPL process, heat transfer mainly occurs through the HTB materials, thereby minimizing heat transfer to the polymeric binder. The thermal conductivity of polymers is generally significantly low because of the scattering of phonons caused by the disordered vibration and rotation of atoms within the polymer chain, which results in slow heat diffusion [70–72]. Consequently, the insufficient heat transfer to PAA during the 10 ms IPL process prevented the complete decomposition of the binder. However, at the excessively high IPL irradiation intensities of 5 J cm−2 or higher, the local temperatures of the HTB materials and SiNPs increased significantly. According to Fourier’s law of heat conduction, the electrode particles generated sufficient heat to trigger the decomposition of nearby binders. TGA was performed under an N2 flow to ensure non-oxidative thermal degradation of the PAA binder (Fig. 4b). The remaining amount of PAA in each electrode was estimated by calculating the weight changes at a temperature range of 150–650 °C. Notably, the PAA binder mostly decomposed at 650 °C (Fig. S4), enabling the determination of the PAA content within the electrode using quantitative calculations (see Note in Fig. S4). Additionally, annealing experiments were conducted on the SN-HTB electrode using a tube furnace at temperatures of 200, 400, and 600 °C under an Ar gas flow to investigate the phenomenon of binder decomposition in both the IPL and conventional slow heat treatments. The electrodes prepared using conventional furnace annealing were designated as SN-HTB F-X (where X =200, 400, and 600) based on the annealing temperature. The TGA results revealed a significant increase in the degradation temperature of PAA, particularly noticeable in the SN-HTB F-200 and SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrodes. This enhancement was attributed to the crosslinking between PAA and the CQDs, which enhanced the thermal stability (Fig. 4c) [73,74]. The degradation of the PAA binder in the SN-HTB 2 J cm−2 electrode was negligible. However, consistent with the FT-IR results, the thermal decomposition of PAA was observed in the SN-HTB 6 J cm−2 electrode. In particular, when the irradiation energy density was 4 J cm−2, only partial decomposition of PAA occurred, and 6.6 wt% PAA remained within the electrode. In the tube furnace annealing experiment, only a small fraction of PAA decomposed at 200 °C, and 9.1 wt% PAA remained within the electrode, whereas almost complete decomposition of PAA was observed at 400 and 600 °C. An interesting finding was observed when the TGA results (Fig. 4d) were compared with the Raman results (Fig. 4e). Specifically, after annealing in a tube furnace at 400 °C, the Ig/Id value of the SN-HTB electrode was 1.38, indicating a slight carbon reduction, whereas PAA was completely decomposed. Surprisingly, in the case of SN-HTB at 4 J cm−2, despite an Ig/Id value of 1.49, indicating a higher degree of carbon reduction than that of the SN-HTB F-400 electrode, 6.6 wt% PAA remained within the electrode. XPS was performed to observe the changes in the chemical bonding environment of carbon depending on the intensity of IPL irradiation. As shown in the C1s spectra in Fig. S6, the C=O and C-O bonds remain unchanged in both SN-HTB Bare and SN-HTB 2 J cm−2. These peaks are attributed to the presence of PAA and conductive carbon within the electrode, indicating no significant reduction of carbon or decomposition of PAA. For the SN-HTB 4 J cm−2, a slight decrease in the C=O and C-O bonds is observed, which is attributed to partial decomposition of PAA and reduction of carbon. For SN-HTB 6 J cm−2, the complete disappearance of the C=O peak demonstrates that PAA is fully decomposed, and there is a substantial reduction in the conductive carbon. This is consistent with the results of TGA and Raman analyses. These results can be ascribed to the selective heating enabled by IPL. IPL, as compared to conventional heat treatment methods that involve longer heat treatment times and uniform heating, offers the advantage of precise and targeted heat generation, allowing for the localized and selective heat treatment of materials. This localized heat treatment can selectively modify electrode materials, such as by carbon reduction. Fig. 5 d depicts the contrasting effects of conventional slow annealing and IPL treatment on the binder and conductive carbon agents within the electrode. The effective use of individual Si particles during electrochemical cycling requires both high conductivity from the conductive carbon materials and their effective distribution within the electrode, particularly among the Si particles, to facilitate electron transfer. Notably, in a Si anode that primarily employs an aqueous binder system, a trade-off exists between the dispersibility and conductivity of carbon materials. Existing methods have inherent limitations in simultaneously enhancing carbon conductivity while maintaining optimal dispersibility in the slurry state. We have fabricated electrodes after the pre-reduction of carbon to investigate how the degree of carbon reduction affects the dispersion of carbon in the electrode. The SN-HTB electrode with pre-reduction process was prepared by pre-heating SiNP/micro HTB (CNTs and AHNS-rGO) composite powder at 400 °C for 1h under an Ar flow in a tube furnace. This pre-treated powder was then used to make a slurry, which was subsequently coated onto copper foil. As shown in Fig. S6, severe aggregation of conductive carbon can be observed in the SN-HTB after pre-reduction. Cross-sectional EPMA analysis also confirmed the presence of carbon agglomerates within the SN-HTB electrode after pre-reduction (Fig. S7). This clearly indicates a trade-off relationship between the dispersibility and conductivity of carbon. The challenges associated with conventional heat treatment methods can be overcome using IPL. The electrical conductivities of the SN-HTB electrodes before and after IPL irradiation were assessed using a four-point probe conductivity measurement system. As shown in Fig. 5a, the conductivities of the SN-HTB electrodes were measured using various irradiation intensities of 0–6 J cm−2. The measured conductivities of SN-HTB Bare, SN-HTB 2 J cm−2, SN-HTB 4 J cm−2, and SN-HTB 6 J cm−2 were 2.91, 4.92, 29.58, and 32.2 S m−1, respectively. Notably, a significant enhancement in electrical conductivity was observed at irradiation intensities exceeding 4 J cm−2, which was attributed to the additional reduction of conductive carbon. This reduction process is considered a pivotal factor that contributes to the improved electrical conductivity of the electrodes. To explore the potential enhancement of the electrical conductivity resulting from the further reduction of conductive carbon, an IPL treatment was applied to the AHNS-rGO film (Fig. 5b). The observed changes in the AHNS-rGO film as the IPL irradiation intensity varied showed a trend similar to that observed in the IPL treatment of the SN-HTB electrodes. FE-SEM analyses were conducted to study the impact of IPL irradiation on the surface morphology of the electrodes (Fig. 5c). The results confirmed the homogeneous dispersion of SiNPs, CNTs, AHNS-rGO, and Super P within the electrodes under all IPL irradiation conditions, with no signs of welding or aggregation. Additionally, no significant alterations in the surface morphology of the electrodes were observed under the various IPL irradiation conditions. Although PAA degrades at 4 J cm−2 or higher, such degradation was not apparent in the FE-SEM analysis. However, the thermal degradation of the PAA binder was elucidated through the TGA measurements. To investigate the effect of IPL on CQDs, we irradiated PAA-CQD films with varying IPL irradiation intensities and subsequently performed XRD analysis. The XRD patterns of these films under different IPL irradiation intensities are shown in Fig. S8. As depicted in the figure, the peak near 19°, corresponding to the graphitic (002) plane, exhibits a gradual increase in intensity with increasing IPL irradiation intensity. This observation indicates that the CQD cores experienced significant heating, leading to notable structural changes. Specifically, the enhancement in peak intensity suggests an increase in crystallinity and the prevalence of sp2 bonding within the CQDs. This phenomenon implies that IPL not only facilitates the thermal processing of CQDs but also enhances their crystallinity, resulting in a more graphitic structure. Such structural improvements are critical, as they likely contribute to the increased electrical conductivity of the CQDs. Enhanced crystallinity within the CQDs improves electron transport properties, which is advantageous for their performance in electrode applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the SN-HTB electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the electrochemical performance of the fabricated electrodes as anodes in LIBs, a 2032-type coin cell was assembled and tested using Li metal as the counter electrode in the half-cells. Galvanostatic charge–discharge tests were performed at a specific current of 0.175 A/g within the voltage range of 0.01–1 V (vs. Li/Li+) for the first two cycles, followed by cyclic stability tests at a current density of 0.5 A/g. Because of the significant volume change that occurs during the charging and discharging of Si, the insufficient mechanical stability of the binders encasing the Si anode engendered an irreversible reaction. The Coulombic efficiency (CE) was calculated to determine the extent of the irreversible reaction during cycling (Fig. 6 b). In a real battery system, in which the battery operates in a closed system under full-cell conditions, the supply of Li is limited, posing a major challenge for suppressing the irreversible reaction caused by the formation of an SEI layer and Li-ion trapping [37,75]. The SiNP electrodes without HTB materials (CNTs, AHNS-rGO, and CQDs) were designated as SN. The SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode exhibited a higher average CE than the SN and SN-HTB Bare electrodes, indicating that the crosslinked network formed from CQDs on the surface of SiNPs could accommodate the volume expansion and contraction during the lithiation and delithiation processes, consequently preventing the exposure of the new SiNP surface to the electrolyte and the formation of a new SEI layer during cycling. However, the average CE in the SN-HTB 6 J cm−2 electrode decreased, presumably owing to the decomposition of all the binders on the SiNP surface, leading to a lower average CE, as compared to the CQD-derived network system in the SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode. This unique binder network structure significantly improved the cyclic stability of the Si anode. As shown in Fig. 6a, the cyclic stability of the SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode was remarkably enhanced, with a sustained capacity exceeding 2000mAh g−1 even after the 100th cycle. EIS was performed to evaluate the impedance changes during extended cycles. As shown in Fig. S10a, the Nyquist plot of the SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode after the 10th cycle shows the smallest semicircle diameter compared to other electrodes, indicating improved electrical conductivity and promoted charge transfer due to the selective thermal treatment effect of IPL on carbon reduction. Notably, after the 100th cycle, while the SN-HTB Bare and SN-HTB 6 J cm−2 electrodes exhibit significant increases in the semicircle diameters, the SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode shows a markedly smaller increase.

Furthermore, a rate capability test was conducted on the SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode; the electrode retained a high specific capacity exceeding 1000mAh g−1 even under a high current density of 3 A/g (charging time < 30 min), as shown in Fig. 6a. This result demonstrates the cyclic stability of the electrode under the high-rate condition of 3 A/g, which demonstrates that using IPL allows for the simultaneous formation of a robust crosslinked PAA-CQD network binder and additional reduction of the carbon additive, resulting in a substantial enhancement in the performance of Si-majority anodes. To gain a deeper understanding of the factors contributing to the cyclic stabilities of the prepared electrodes, bulk-scale morphological analyses of their surfaces before and after cycling (50 cycles) were conducted, as shown in Fig. 6c and d. Notably, after cycling, the SEM image of the SN-HTB Bare electrode revealed conspicuous large cracks (Fig. 6c), indicating the inadequacy of the PAA binder for mitigating the electrode collapse resulting from the substantial volume change of the Si particles. Such large cracks during cycling lead to a reduction in the particle–particle contact, resulting in decreased sequential capacity. In contrast, the SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode showed no large cracks after cycling (Fig. 6d), suggesting that the proposed IPL-treated PAA-CQD network binder system effectively suppressed intensive structural changes and cracking.

Additionally, a substantially thick SEI layer was observed on the SN-HTB Bare electrode, which resulted from the newly exposed Si surface during continuous lithiation/delithiation cycling. The formation of this new SEI layer leads to the persistent consumption of Li and a reduction in the electrical conductivity of the electrode owing to the thickening of the non-conductive SEI. In contrast, a relatively thin SEI layer was formed on the SN-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode, indicating that the crosslinked PAA-CQD binder system effectively held the pulverized Si particles and prevented repeated SEI formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of IPL to SiMPs for versatility confirmation
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the scalability and practical applicability of IPL, SiMP electrodes were employed in addition to the SiNP electrodes. Bulk graphite was used as the HTB material to accommodate the microscale particles, thus enhancing heat transfer on a larger scale, as compared to AHNS-rGO. CNTs were also incorporated as an HTB material. Fig. S9a shows that the SiMPs and microscale HTB materials were homogeneously dispersed within the electrode. In the enlarged FE-SEM image in Fig. S9b, the SM-HTB electrodes exhibited no significant surface morphology changes at different IPL irradiation intensities of 0–5 J cm−2. However, a noticeable surface morphology change was observed in SM-HTB 6 J cm−2, which was attributed to the decomposition and partial carbonization of PAA. Cyclic stability tests were conducted on the SM-HTB electrode under different IPL irradiation intensities, as shown in Fig. 6e. The cyclic stability of the SM-HTB electrodes exhibited a trend similar to that of the SN-HTB electrodes. The SM-HTB 4 J cm−2 electrode exhibited the highest cyclic stability and maintained a high capacity exceeding 2000mAh g−1 after 70 cycles. However, unlike the cycling behavior of the SN-HTB electrodes, the SM-HTB 6 J cm−2 electrode exhibited a significant capacity decrease during the initial cycle. This trend was attributed to the SiMPs having larger particles than SiNPs, making particle pulverization and electrode particle delamination more severe, which is a major challenge hindering the commercialization of cost-effective SiMPs. The SM-HTB 6 J cm−2 electrode, in which the binder was completely decomposed, exhibited steeper capacity fading owing to significant Li ion loss in the initial cycle. Moreover, the SiMPs, as compared to the SiNPs, exhibited a higher ICE when the IPL irradiation intensity was 4 J cm−2 or less because the SiMPs had a smaller specific surface area (see Fig. 6f and Fig. S12) and hence formed a less extensive initial SEI layer. Notably, the ICE value at an intensity of 6 J cm−2 did not prevent the delamination and isolation of the SiMP electrode, indicating a considerable loss of Li ions in the initial cycle. These findings suggest that the role of the binder was more crucial in SiMPs than in SiNPs. Consequently, along with SiNPs, SiMPs were verified as viable for use in IPL processing owing to their cost-effectiveness and greater availability in the market. This study has significant implications on multiple fronts, encompassing the following aspects: (i) the introduction of a selective heat treatment technology applicable to the R2R process, which is widely employed in commercial battery electrode manufacturing; (ii) incorporating conductive carbon additives with an inherent tradeoff between dispersibility and conductivity requires a certain content level to establish an effective electrical pathway within the electrode. Accordingly, IPL is a potent technique that enhances electrode conductivity while mitigating binder decomposition, suggesting the potential for reducing the content of conductive materials using a mere 10 ms IPL irradiation time; (iii) ensuring optimal electrode performance requires considering not only the optical and thermal properties of the material but also a profound understanding of battery engineering; (iv) the demonstrated applicability of the SiMPs indicates the adaptability of this approach to diverse battery applications.

This study proposed a novel approach using IPL to achieve a concurrent reduction of the conductive carbon additive and modification of the binder in Si anodes while effectively mitigating binder degradation using the conventional slow-heating method. These high-performance Si anodes can be systematically and meticulously designed, leveraging the optical and thermal properties inherent in each electrode constituent in conjunction with the distinctive attributes offered by the extremely short irradiation of visible light in IPL processing. Introducing microscale HTBs (CNTs and AHNS-rGO) established a dominant pathway for efficient heat transfer within the electrode, enabling uniform heat treatment during the IPL processing of the designed SiNP electrode and effectively alleviating binder decomposition. Furthermore, the CQDs served a dual role as nanoscale HTBs and crosslinking agents within the PAA binder system, thereby accommodating the volumetric expansion of the SiNPs during electrochemical cycling. The applicability of IPL extends beyond SiNP electrodes to SiMP electrodes. The IPL technology introduced in this study is highly suitable for industrial applications, as it enables large-area processing without special atmospheric conditions and offers a short processing time of 10 ms. Particularly noteworthy is its potential to reduce the conductive agent content, which typically entails a trade-off between dispersibility and electrical conductivity, thereby facilitating the realization of a higher electrode energy density. This result provides comprehensive insights into the selective heat treatment of IPL and its potential application in alloy anodes. Employing this post-treatment IPL electrode technology holds great promise for successfully integrating Si anodes into large-scale battery manufacturing processes. This breakthrough in selective heat treatment using IPL opens new avenues for the incorporation of emerging technologies into battery electrode engineering.
